sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: fat is 0g. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a tuber, the question is whether the starch is behaving itself.
sections.subtitleText: fat is 0g. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a tuber, the question is whether the starch is behaving itself.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: carbs is 17.4g. starch is doing most of the heavy lifting here. for tubers, the carb side decides whether the food feels stable or flimsy.
sections.subtitleText: carbs is 17.4g. starch is doing most of the heavy lifting here. for tubers, the carb side decides whether the food feels stable or flimsy.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: protein is 2g. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. protein is usually limited here, so the other sections have to carry more.

sections.narration: vitamin a at 0% DV, vitamin c at 6% DV stand out most. that is barely moving the needle.

sections.title: Minerals
sections.narration: minerals are basically not part of the case here.
sections.subtitleText: minerals are basically not part of the case here.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: pros first. practical staple carb source because tubers are often judged by how usable they are day to day. whole-food carb package because that matters because it is a cleaner baseline than heavily refined carbs. can be quite filling because a useful practical strength when the preparation stays sensible.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: on the weaker side. still a carb-heavy category because that is the core ceiling tubers have to manage. preparation changes outcomes a lot because what you do to a tuber can make or break it. protein support is weak because tubers still need help from the rest of the meal.
